title: Numbers & Time: Making Plans Clearly
objectives: Ask and answer what time it is.
Say your age using tengo ... años.
Talk about days of the week, today, tomorrow, and yesterday.
Make simple plans with a day and time.
objective: Use numbers, clock time, days, and calendar phrases to make simple plans with Carlos.

vocabulary.tip: Useful for phone numbers, room numbers, and counting.
Uno changes to un before masculine nouns: un café.
Useful for simple meeting times.
Use veinte for 20; numbers 21-29 start with veinti-.
For 31-39, use treinta y plus the smaller number.
Do not pronounce it like English quarantine; keep Spanish vowels clean.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: My father is forty years old.

vocabulary.tip: Notice the cinc- beginning, like cinco.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: My mom is fifty years old.

vocabulary.tip: Keep the stress in the middle: se-SEN-ta.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Hay sesenta minutos en una hora.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: There are sixty minutes in an hour.

vocabulary.tip: Setenta and sesenta look similar, so listen carefully.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: My grandfather is seventy years old.

vocabulary.tip: Ocho is hiding inside ochenta.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: My grandmother is eighty years old.

vocabulary.tip: Nueve is related to noventa.
Use cien for exactly 100.
Use hora for time and for an hour.
Reloj can mean a clock or a watch.
Use this to ask the current time.
Use es la only for one o'clock.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Es la una de la tarde.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: It is one in the afternoon.

vocabulary.tip: Use son las for every hour except one.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Son las tres de la tarde.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: It is three in the afternoon.

vocabulary.tip: Use after the hour for fifteen minutes past.
Use after the hour for thirty minutes past.
Use before the next hour for fifteen minutes before.
Use for morning times.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Trabajo a las ocho de la mañana.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I work at eight in the morning.

vocabulary.tip: Use for afternoon and early evening times.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Tomamos café a las tres de la tarde.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: We have coffee at three in the afternoon.

vocabulary.tip: Use for night times.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Ceno a las ocho de la noche.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I eat dinner at eight at night.

vocabulary.tip: Use hoy for the current day.
Mañana can mean tomorrow or morning; context tells you which.
Use ayer for the day before today.
Days of the week are lowercase in Spanish.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I work from Monday to Friday.

vocabulary.tip: Use semana for week.
Use mes for month.
Año has ñ. Do not confuse it with ano, which is a different word.
Spanish uses tener for age: literally, I have ... years.
Cumpleaños is used for birthday and stays the same in singular.
Tarde can mean late or afternoon depending on context.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Me levanto temprano, pero ceno tarde.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I wake up early, but I eat dinner late.

vocabulary.tip: Use these to order a simple schedule.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Now I study and afterward I eat dinner.

vocabulary.tip: Use with simple routines.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I always practice; I never arrive late.

grammar.topic: Numbers, time, age, and simple schedules
grammar.explanation: Spanish uses set patterns for time and age. Use es la una for one o'clock, son las for other times, tener for age, and lowercase days of the week for schedules.

notes.title: Es la vs son las
notes.text: Use es la for one o'clock and son las for every other hour: Es la una. = It is one o'clock. Son las tres. = It is three o'clock. Practice: ask Carlos the time and answer with one o'clock, then three o'clock. Watch out: do not say es las tres.

notes.title: Telling time
notes.text: Use the hour plus y cuarto, y media, or menos cuarto: Son las tres y cuarto. = It is quarter past three. Son las seis y media. = It is half past six. Practice: say 3:15 and 6:30. Watch out: remember las before most hours.

notes.title: Saying your age with tener
notes.text: Spanish uses tener for age: Tengo veinte años. = I am twenty years old. Carlos tiene treinta años. = Carlos is thirty years old. Practice: tell Carlos your age. Watch out: do not say soy veinte años.

notes.title: Today, tomorrow, yesterday
notes.text: Hoy means today, mañana can mean tomorrow, and ayer means yesterday: Hoy es lunes. = Today is Monday. Mañana veo a Carlos. = Tomorrow I see Carlos. Practice: say one sentence with each word. Watch out: mañana can also mean morning.

notes.title: Days of the week
notes.text: Spanish days are lowercase: lunes, martes, miércoles, jueves, viernes, sábado, domingo. Hoy es martes. = Today is Tuesday. Practice: say today's day and tomorrow's day. Watch out: do not capitalize days unless they start a sentence.

notes.title: Simple schedules
notes.text: Use a las plus the time for schedules: Trabajo a las ocho. = I work at eight. Ceno a las siete. = I eat dinner at seven. Practice: say when you wake up and when you eat dinner. Watch out: use a las, not en las.

notes.title: AM and PM expressions
notes.text: Use de la mañana, de la tarde, and de la noche for AM and PM: A las ocho de la mañana. = At eight in the morning. A las ocho de la noche. = At eight at night. Practice: make one morning plan and one evening plan. Watch out: Spanish does not always say AM or PM in casual speech.

examples.spanish: Son las tres de la tarde.
examples.english: It is three in the afternoon.

examples.spanish: Nos vemos el sábado a las diez.
examples.english: We will see each other on Saturday at ten.

grammar.practicePrompt: Tell Carlos your age, today's day, and one plan with a day and time.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: What are you doing on Saturday?

lines.english: I work from Monday to Friday.

lines.english: Very good. And do you rest on Sunday?

lines.english: I eat dinner at seven at night.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Nos vemos mañana a las diez?
lines.english: Will we see each other tomorrow at ten?

lines.spanish: Me levanto temprano y estudio después.
lines.english: I wake up early and study afterward.

phrases.note: Listen for son las before the hour.

phrases.english: I eat dinner at seven at night.

pronunciation.focus: Tens, reloj, años, mañana, rolled R, ñ, and stress patterns.
tips: The single r in treinta and cuarenta is tapped lightly, not pronounced like English r.
The rr sound is stronger, but this lesson mostly needs a light tapped r.
The ñ in años and mañana sounds like ny in canyon.
Words ending in -enta usually stress the middle syllable: cua-REN-ta, cin-CUEN-ta.
Do not add an English-style w sound to reloj; start with a light Spanish r.

items.note: Stress trein gently; keep the r light.
Use a light Spanish r and finish with a clear j sound.
The ñ sounds like ny; do not say anos.
The ñ sounds like ny; context decides morning or tomorrow.

pronunciationExercises.note: Stress trein gently; keep the r light.
Use a light Spanish r and finish with a clear j sound.
The ñ sounds like ny; do not say anos.
The ñ sounds like ny; context decides morning or tomorrow.

culture.title: Time, age, and schedules in real life
culture.text: Spanish speakers may use the 24-hour clock for transportation, appointments, school, and official times, while the 12-hour clock is common in everyday conversation. In Spain, lunch and dinner often happen later than in North America, so dinner at nine or ten can be normal. Spanish uses tengo años for age because age is expressed as something you have. Days of the week are lowercase. Birthdays often include family greetings, food, songs, and warm messages. Punctuality expectations vary by country, region, event, and relationship, so confirm the exact time when plans matter.

cultureNotes: Use the 24-hour clock for official times and the 12-hour clock in many casual conversations.
Meal times in Spain can be later than in North America.
Spanish uses tener for age: tengo treinta años.
Days of the week are lowercase in Spanish.
For plans, confirm the day and time clearly.

nativeSpeech.title: How Carlos would say it naturally
notes: Qué hora es? is the standard way to ask the time.
A qué hora? means At what time?
Nos vemos el sábado a las diez is a natural plan.
For casual plans, people often skip AM or PM if the context is clear.
Tengo treinta años sounds natural; soy treinta años does not.

commonMistakes.explanation: Use son las for every hour except one.

commonMistakes.correction: Say a las tres or son las tres.
commonMistakes.explanation: Most time expressions need las before the hour.

commonMistakes.correction: Use context: mañana can mean tomorrow, and la mañana means the morning.
commonMistakes.explanation: Mañana trabajo means tomorrow I work. De la mañana means in the morning.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using PM directly in every casual sentence.
commonMistakes.correction: Use de la tarde or de la noche when you need clarity.
commonMistakes.explanation: Spanish commonly says tres de la tarde or ocho de la noche.

quiz.prompt: What does Tengo veinte años mean?
Choose the Spanish for It is one o'clock.
Which phrase means in the afternoon?
Carlos asks: A qué hora cenas? Choose the best answer.

options: Nos vemos el sábado a las diez.
Nos vemos a sábado de diez.
quiz.answer: Nos vemos el sábado a las diez.

quiz.prompt: What does mañana mean in Mañana trabajo?
Translate: Son las tres de la tarde.
quiz.answer: It is three in the afternoon.

quiz.prompt: Translate: I am thirty years old.
You want coffee with Carlos at 3 PM. What do you say?
options: Tomamos café a las tres de la tarde.
quiz.answer: Tomamos café a las tres de la tarde.

speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Uno, dos, tres, cuatro, cinco, seis, siete, ocho, nueve, diez, once, doce, trece, catorce, quince, dieciséis, diecisiete, dieciocho, diecinueve, veinte.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Tell Carlos what time you wake up and eat dinner.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Me levanto a las siete de la mañana y ceno a las siete de la noche.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Arrange a meeting with Carlos using a day and time.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Carlos, nos vemos el sábado a las tres de la tarde.

realLifeMission.title: Real Life Mission
realLifeMission.mission: Arrange to meet Carlos for coffee. Include a greeting, the day, the time, your age, and a goodbye.
successCriteria: Start with a greeting from Lessons 1 or 2.
Say your age with tengo ... años.
Choose a day of the week.
Choose a clear coffee time using a las.
End with a natural goodbye, such as nos vemos or hasta luego.

review.summary: You can now count from 0 to 100, ask and tell the time, say your age, name days of the week, and make simple plans with a day and time.
canDo: Use numbers for age, time, and simple schedules.
Ask Qué hora es? and answer with es la or son las.
Say your age with tengo ... años.
Use hoy, mañana, ayer, and days of the week.
Arrange a simple meeting with Carlos.
selfCheck: Can I count from 1 to 20 without stopping?
Can I say my age correctly with tengo?
Can I say today's day and tomorrow's day?
Can I arrange coffee with Carlos using a day and time?
review.nextStep: Now that you can talk about time and schedules, you're ready to use Spanish while shopping in real stores and markets.
